results.possibleIdentity: Paul Vanders (alternatively spelled Paul Vandes in some contexts) was an artist and inventor active in New York City during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is primarily recognized for his Brutalist-style metal wall sculptures and reliefs, which were often constructed from hammered, folded, and torch-cut metals such as brass, copper, chrome, and aluminum. His works from the 1970s are characterized by abstract and geometric patterns, sometimes mounted on mirrored or wooden backgrounds, and are frequently compared to or associated with the work of fellow Brutalist artist Stephen Chun. Beyond his artistic output, Vanders demonstrated a strong interest in practical engineering and design; in January 1969, he filed a patent (US Patent 3,526,317) for a 'Combination Drinking-Cup Wrapped Product Package' while living at 345 East 58th Street in New York. This dual identity as both a creator of fine art and a patented inventor matches the profile of participants in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, a non-profit established in 1966 in New York to foster collaborations between the artistic and scientific communities.

results.possibleIdentity: Kenneth (Ken) Marsh is an artist and author who was a pioneer in the independent video and video art movements of the late 1960s and 1970s. Originally a painter and printmaker, he also worked with light shows during the late 1960s before transitioning to video technology. In 1970, along with Elliot Glass, he co-founded the People's Video Theater (PVT) in New York City, which focused on using video for community dialogue, journalism, and social change. Marsh was a significant figure in the alternative media scene, contributing to the seminal magazine Radical Software and authoring the influential book "Independent Video: A Complete Guide to the Physics, Operation, and Application of the New Medium for the Student, Artist, and Community Activist" in 1974. He later established Woodstock Community Video (WCV) in 1973 and was a resident artist at the Experimental Television Center in 1975–76. His work often overlapped with the goals of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as he utilized emerging technological tools to create new forms of communication and artistic expression.
